title: Enantiomeric separation of N-protected amino acids by non-aqueous capillary electrophoresis using quinine or tert-butyl carbamoylated quinine as chiral additive.
abstract:
Enantiomeric Separation of N-Protected Amino
Acids by Non-Aqueous Capillary Electrophoresis
Using Quinine or tert-Butyl Carbamoylated Quinine
as Chiral Additive
VERONIQUE PIETTE,1 MICHAEL LAMMERHOFER,2 WOLFGANG LINDNER,2 AND
JACQUES CROMMEN3*
1Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, University of Namur, Rue de Bruxelles, 61, B-5000 Namur, Belgium
2Institute of Analytical Chemistry, University of Vienna, Wa¨hringerstrasse, 38, A-1090 Vienna, Austria
3Department of Analytical Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Institute of Pharmacy, University of Lie`ge, CHU, B36,
B-4000 Lie`ge 1, Belgium
ABSTRACT
A capillary electrophoretic (CE) method for the enantioseparation of
N-protected chiral amino acids was developed using quinine and tert-butyl carbam-
oylated quinine as chiral selectors added to nonaqueous electrolyte solutions (NACE). A
series of various N-derivatized amino acids were tested as chiral selectands, and in order
to optimize the CE enantioseparation of these compounds, different parameters were
investigated: the nature of the organic solvent, the combination of different solvents, the
nature and the concentration of the background electrolyte, the selector concentration,
the capillary temperature, and the applied voltage. The influence of these factors on the
separation of the analyte enantiomers and the electroosmotic flow was studied. Gener-
ally, with tert-butyl carbamoylated quinine as chiral selector, better enantioseparations
were achieved than with unmodified quinine. Optimum experimental conditions were
found with a buffer made of 12.5 mM ammonia, 100 mM octanoic acid, and 10 mM
tert-butyl carbamoylated quinine in an ethanol–methanol mixture (60:40 v/v). Under
these conditions, DNB-Leu enantiomers could be separated with a selectivity factor (a)
of 1.572 and a resolution (Rs) of 64.3; a plate number (N) of 127,000 and an asymmetry
factor (As) of 0.93 were obtained for the first migrating enantiomer. Chirality 11:622–
630, 1999.
© 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
KEY WORDS: nonaqueous capillary electrophoresis; enantiomeric separations, tert-
butyl carbamoylated quinine; ion-pairing; N-protected amino acids
INTRODUCTION
Capillary electrophoresis (CE) may be useful in the de-
velopment of new high-performance liquid chromato-
graphic (HPLC) chiral stationary phases and in the under-
standing of chiral recognition mechanisms. Most often in
CE, chiral additives are employed for enantiomeric sepa-
rations.1–4 Unfortunately, many of the chiral selectors
(SOs) successfully applied in chiral HPLC have only lim-
ited solubility in the aqueous buffers that are generally
used in CE. In the past few years, a growing interest in the
use of nonaqueous media has been observed since Wal-
broehl and Jorgenson5 showed the CE separation of stan-
dard organic bases in acetonitrile. Clearly the introduction
of nonaqueous media has extended the applicability of
CE.6–17 A wider range of dielectric constants, viscosities,
polarities, densities, and acid/base equilibria becomes
available. All these parameters may influence selectivity
and therefore resolution. By selecting a suitable medium, a
wider range of solutes will be soluble, hence making them
amenable to CE analysis. Nonaqueous CE (NACE), em-
ploying small amounts of a well-characterized chiral addi-
tive, offers an attractive alternative for screening potential
chiral stationary phase selectors for HPLC and for studying
the interactions between the selector and various types of
analytes.18,19 Among chiral selectors, quinine has been
used as a chiral solvating agent in NMR studies,20 as a
chiral ion-pairing agent for enantioseparation in HPLC21,22
as well as in capillary electrochromatography (CEC)23 and
as a chiral stationary phase ligand for HPLC.24,25 Recently,
carbamoylated quinine and quinidine-based chiral station-
ary phases were found to be highly stereoselective for the
direct resolution of chiral acids using mixtures of aqueous
buffers and methanol or acetonitrile as mobile phases.26–31
These new chiral selectors (SOs) can be classified as weak
chiral anion exchangers due to the tertiary amine within
the quinuclidine substituent that is protonated at the usual
working pH of the mobile phase. The primary ionic inter-
*Correspondence to: Jacques Crommen, Department of Analytical Phar-
maceutical Chemistry, Institute of Pharmacy, University of Lie`ge, CHU,
B36, B-4000 Lie`ge 1, Belgium.
Received for publication 27 October 1998; Accepted 7 February 1999
CHIRALITY 11:622–630 (1999)
© 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
 action between the anionic solutes (selectands, SAs) and
the SO is significantly accompanied by additional intermo-
lecular interactions as hydrogen bonding, dipole–dipole,
p–p, hydrophobic, and steric interactions. These simulta-
neously acting multiple interactions can be seen as a basis
for the high stereoselectivity of these SOs. Moreover, to
increase stereoselectivity, the introduction of a bulky alkyl
substituent at the carbamate function turned out to be ad-
vantageous.26,27,32
In this study, in order to develop a CE system for the
rapid screening of different kinds of quinine derivatives as
SOs, such as quinine (QN) and tert-butyl carbamoylated
quinine (tBuCQN) (see Fig. 1), were used as chiral addi-
tives in NACE. A series of different N-protected amino acid
derivatives including Bz- (benzoyl), DNB- (3,5-dinitroben-
zoyl), and DNZ- (3,5-dinitrobenzyloxycarbonyl)amino ac-
ids (see Fig. 2) were tested as chiral selectands.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Apparatus
All experiments were performed on a Spectraphoresis
1000 CE instrument (Spectraphysics, San Jose, CA)
equipped with an automatic injector, an autosampler, a vari-
able wavelength UV-visible detector (190–800 nm) and a
temperature control system (15–60°C). Electrophoretic
separations were carried out on uncoated fused-silica cap-
illaries, 50 µm ID and 44 cm length (37 cm to the detector),
provided by Supelco (Bellefonte, PA). At the beginning of
each working day, the capillary was washed with running
buffer for 15 min, while after each injection the capillary
was washed with buffer for 4 min. The injections were
made at the cathodic side and the applied voltage was −20
kV (reversed polarity mode). The normal polarity mode
(+20 kV) was only used to measure the electroosmotic
mobility µEOF, which was directed to the cathode. The UV
detection (at the anodic side) was performed at 214 nm.
Injections were made in the hydrodynamic mode for a pe-
riod of 5 s (corresponding to 13.3 nL) and the capillary was
thermostatted at 25°C unless otherwise stated. For the first
electrophoretic experiments, a buffer of ammonium ac-
etate in organic solvents was used. For further experi-
ments an electrolyte solution containing quinine or its car-
bamoylated derivative was employed as running buffer.
The same electrolyte solution without the UV absorbing
selector was introduced in the reservoir at the anodic (de-
tector) side to avoid high UV absorbance background
when the analytes were detected. The apparent pH (pHa)
of the nonaqueous electrolyte solutions was measured by
means of a Delta Model 345 pH-meter (Mettler, Healstead,
U.K.). The resolution (Rs) and the plate number (N) were
calculated according to the standard expressions based on
peak width at half-height.33 The selectivity factor (a) was
expressed as the ratio of the effective mobilities µe of two
enantiomers. The asymmetry factor (As) was determined
using the expression As = w(0.10)/2F(0.10), where w(0.10)
is the width of peak at 10% of height and F(0.10) is the
distance between the top and the front edge of the peak at
10% of height.
Reagents
QN was obtained by Sigma (St. Louis, MO) and tBuCQN
was synthesized according to a standard procedure de-
scribed elsewhere.34 Buffers were prepared with ammo-
nium acetate of analytical grade obtained from Merck
(Darmstadt, Germany). The organic solvents were of
HPLC grade: absolute ethanol from Merck, methanol and
acetonitrile from Fisher Scientific (Leicestershire, U.K.).
Butyric, hexanoic, heptanoic, mandelic, octanoic, nona-
noic, and decanoic acids were from Sigma. Acetic acid was
from Merck, and 25% ammonia solution was from Carlo
Erba (Rodano, Italy). The racemic and enantiomerically
pure amino acids were purchased from Sigma. Bz and
DNB derivatives were synthesized according to standard
procedures30 except DNB-Leu and DNB-Phg which were
obtained from Sigma. To synthesize the DNZ compounds,
aqueous solutions of amino acids were derivatized with
3,5-dinitrobenzyl chloroformate.31 The sample solutions
were prepared by dissolving each amino acid derivative at
a concentration of 50 µg/mL in methanol. Benzyl alcohol
from Sigma (0.01% solution in methanol) was used as neu-
tral marker to visualize the electroosmotic breakthrough
time. Buffers and samples were filtered through a Polypure
polypropylene membrane filter (0.2 µm; Alltech, Laarne,
Belgium) before use.
Fig. 1. Chiral selectors: (1) quinine; (2) tert-butyl carbamoylated qui-
nine.
Fig. 2. N-protected amino acid derivatives: (1) Bz (benzoyl); (2) DNB
(3,5-dinitrobenzoyl); (3) DNZ (3,5-dinitrobenzyloxycarbonyl).
ENANTIOSEPARATION OF N-PROTECTED AMINO ACIDS BY NONAQUEOUS CE

Fig. 1. Chiral selectors: (1) quinine; (2) tert-butyl carbamoylated qui-
Fig. 2. N-protected amino acid derivatives: (1) Bz (benzoyl); (2) DNB

ENANTIOSEPARATION OF N-PROTECTED AMINO ACIDS BY NONAQUEOUS CE
Preliminary Studies without Chiral Selector
Selection of the Organic Solvent and the Electrolyte
In order to select experimental condi-
tions allowing the detection of the SAs in a reasonable
analysis time, two NACE systems using methanol or etha-
nol as organic solvent were first studied. The background
electrolyte was ammonium acetate; DNB-Leu and DNB-
Phg were selected as model compounds. The influence of
the electrolyte concentration on the electroosmotic mobil-
ity (µEOF) and the effective mobilities (µe) of the analytes
was studied. These results are presented in Fig. 3.
The lower viscosity (h = 0.54 cP) and the higher dielec-
tric constant (« = 32.7) of methanol led to more significant
electroosmotic flow and electromigration of the analytes
than in ethanol (h = 1.07 cP, « = 24.6).11 As expected,
increasing the electrolyte concentration decreased both
electroosmotic and effective mobilities. Accordingly, a
buffer containing the lower ammonium acetate concentra-
tion (12.5 mM) in methanol was selected.
It is worth noting that when the pHa of this achiral me-
dium (7.9) was adjusted to 9 and 10 with ammonia or to 7
with acetic acid, the electroosmotic flow was found to in-
crease slightly with increasing pHa while effective mobili-
ties were almost unaffected in this pHa range.
Under the selected experimental conditions, the tertiary
quinuclidine amine within the chiral SO (see Fig. 1) is
protonated and may interact with the negatively charged
SAs by ionic interaction to form electrically neutral ion
pairs.18,19 Due to their opposite charge, free SO and SAs
exhibit countercurrent-like electrophoretic migration and
their overall velocity is also influenced by µEOF. On the
other hand, the ion pairs have no electrophoretic migration
and move with the µEOF (cathodic flow). Thus, significant
mobility differences are obtained between free and com-
plexed SA species, a fact that gives rise to high enantiose-
lectivity, provided that different ion pair formation con-
stants for the (R)- and (S)-enantiomers of the SAs (based
on the aforementioned enantioselective intermolecular in-
teractions) are obtained. Overall, different effective migra-
tion rates should be observed, which are the basis for the
Influence of Quinine Concentration.
different concentrations (1–5 mM) to 12.5 mM ammonium
acetate in methanol buffer. The influence of the QN con-
centration of µEOF, µe, and on the separation parameters a
(enantioselectivity) and Rs (resolution) for the correspond-
ing enantiomers (S) and (R) is presented in Table 1.
Current instability was observed at QN concentrations
Rather poor enantioseparation was observed for the two
amino acid derivatives examined. However, higher mobil-
ity differences and selectivity values for analyte enantio-
mers were obtained at higher SO concentrations, e.g. a
(DNB-Leu) = 1.006 at 1 mM and 1.013 at 2.5 mM SO con-
centration. This SO was also tested in the same concentra-
tion range using 25 mM ammonium acetate buffer: again,
2.5 mM was also found to be the best concentration, but
resolution values were lower. Compared to the results ob-
tained without SO, µEOF was only slightly affected by the
TABLE 1. Influence of QN concentration on enantioresolution*
*Buffer: 12.5 mM ammonium acetate in methanol. Other conditions as described in Section 2.1; c.i., current instability: –, no enantiomeric separation
Fig. 3. Influence of the electrolyte concentration in
methanolic and ethanolic buffers. Buffer: ammonium
Comparison of Quinine and tert-Butyl Carbamoylated
amino acid derivatives examined was studied with each
chiral SO (QN and tBuCQN as SOs) under the selected
operating conditions (12.5 mM ammonium acetate and 2.5
mM SO in methanol). The results are presented in Table 2.
With QN, enantiomeric separations were achieved with
the given nonoptimized conditions for DNB-Leu, DNB-
aAbu, DNB-bAbu, and DNZ-aAbu, but chiral resolution
values were very low. Compared to DNZ- and Bz-
derivatives, the structure of the DNB-derivatives seems to
be more favorable with respect to the chiral discrimination
capability of QN as SO. The (S)-enantiomer migrated first.
With tBuCQN, higher resolution values were obtained,
resulting in the complete enantioseparation of DNB-Leu,
DNB-aAbu, and DNB-bAbu. In addition, DNB-Phe, DNZ-
Leu, and DNZ-Phe were partly enantioseparated. The ad-
ditional tert-butyl substituent in the SO and in particular the
carbamate function, that may serve as hydrogen donor–
acceptor, has obviously a favorable effect on enantioselec-
tivity. It is also worth noting that in the presence of
tBuCQN, the migration order was reversed compared to
TABLE 2. Enantioresolution of amino acid derivatives with QN and tBuCQN as selectors*
*Buffer: 12.5 mM ammonium acetate and 2.5 mM QN or tBuCQN in methanol. Other conditions as described in Section 2.1; –, no enantiomeric separation
Fig. 4. Enantioseparation of DNB-Leu. Buffer: 12.5
mM ammonium acetate in methanol, containing no or
2.5 mM concentration of selector.
ENANTIOSEPARATION OF N-PROTECTED AMINO ACIDS BY NONAQUEOUS CE
QN: the (R)-enantiomer of the N-acyl- and N-oxycarbonyl-
amino acids migrated before their (S)-antipodes. This re-
versal of migration order, which has also been observed in
the corresponding chromatographic enantioseparations
with CSPs based on QN and tBuCQN as chiral SOs, is due
to a change in the relative binding affinities of the corre-
sponding SA enantiomers and indicates a change in the
Figure 4 presents the electropherograms of DNB-Leu
without SO, with QN, and with tBuCQN. The shorter mi-
gration times for the SA enantiomers in the presence of
tBuCQN are related to the significant decrease of µEOF
Influence of the Capillary Temperature and the Ap-
The enantioresolution of the model com-
pounds, DNB-Leu and DNB-Phg, was studied with QN at
the selected buffer concentration. The capillary tempera-
ture was varied from 15 to 25°C (applied voltage: −20 kV),
and the applied voltage varied from −15 to −30 kV (capillary
temperature: 25°C). Table 3 presents the results obtained
at the different temperatures and Table 4 presents the re-
sults at the different applied voltages.
As expected, enantioselectivity was found to improve
when the temperature was decreased (Table 3). A tempera-
ture of 15°C (the lowest temperature which can be reached
with the instrument) was selected for further studies. The
increase in µEOF and µe observed with high voltages does
not improve chiral resolution significantly (Table 4). How-
ever, because the best efficiencies were attained at −25 kV
(44,000 plates for DNB-Leu and 58,000 plates for DNB-
Phg), this voltage was selected for further investigations.
Influence of the Addition of Acetonitrile to the Back-
In achiral conditions, buffer contain-
ing 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 mM ammonium acetate were
studied using 20% and 40% of acetonitrile in methanol (re-
sults not detailed here). Due to the addition of acetonitrile
(h = 0.33 cP, « = 37.511) an increase in µEOF and a smaller
increase in µe resulting in longer migration times were
observed at each concentration. With 40% acetonitrile, mi-
gration times were longer than 90 min. Using 12.5 mM
acetate buffer containing 2.5 mM tBuCQN at 15°C and −25
kV, the influence of the addition of 10–30% of acetonitrile
on µEOF, t, µe, As, N for the (R) and (S) enantiomers, and
on measured a and Rs values is presented in Table 5.
The increase in migration times (>90 min with 30% ace-
tonitrile) was accompanied by a decrease in a and Rs. The
alteration of enantioselectivity can be explained by the fact
that intermolecular interactions can be influenced by sol-
vation effects, e.g., p–p interaction can be balanced by
acetonitrile due to solvation of aromatic moieties. More-
over, the increase in µEOF has a negative influence on ef-
ficiency and peak symmetry and thus on Rs.
Influence of the Addition of Ethanol to the Background
Table 6 presents the migration and separa-
tion data obtained with the same electrolyte composition
(12.5 mM acetate and 2.5 mM tBuCQN) but containing up
to 70% of ethanol, instead of acetonitrile.
TABLE 3. Influence of capillary temperature on enantioresolution*
*Buffer: 12.5 mM ammonium acetate and 2.5 mM QN in methanol. Applied voltage: −20 kV. Other conditions as described in Section 2.1.
TABLE 4. Influence of applied voltage on enantioresolution*
*Buffer: 12.5 mM ammonium acetate and 2.5 mM QN in methanol. Capillary temperature: 25°C. Other conditions as described in Section 2.1.
TABLE 6. Influence on enantioresolution of the addition of ethanol to the background electrolyte*
*Buffer: 12.5 mM ammonium acetate and 2.5 mM tBuCQN in methanol-ethanol mixture. Capillary temperature: 15°C. Applied voltage: −25 kV. Other
conditions as described in Section 2.1.
TABLE 7. Influence on enantioresolution of the nature of the background electrolyte acid*
*Buffer: 12.5 mM of the selected acid and ammonia and 2.5 mM tBuCQN in methanol–ethanol (60:40). Capillary temperature: 15°C. Applied voltage: −25
kV. Other conditions as described in Section 2.1.
TABLE 5. Influence on enantioresolution of the addition of acetonitrile to the background electrolyte*
*Buffer: 12.5 mM ammonium acetate and 2.5 mM tBuCQN in methanol–acetonitrile mixture. Capillary temperature: 15°C. Applied voltage: −25 kV. Other
conditions as described in Section 2.1; n.d.,not determined.
ENANTIOSEPARATION OF N-PROTECTED AMINO ACIDS BY NONAQUEOUS CE
By addition of ethanol (higher viscosity and lower dielec-
tric constant than methanol) µEOF and µe decreased simul-
taneously so that migration times were not significantly
affected. In contrast to acetonitrile, a higher percentage of
ethanol gave rise to higher selectivity and resolution val-
ues. A less polar solvent than methanol may provide a
better environment for achieving ion-pair interactions: the
enantioseparation should improve and thus the separation
parameters will increase. This favorable effect reached a
maximum between 50% and 70% ethanol. The selectivity
improvement was accompanied by an increase in efficiency
and peak symmetry, giving rise to a significant enhance-
ment of enantiomeric resolution.
Influence of the Nature of the Acidic Component of the
So far, peaks presented a tail-
ing due to a mismatch between the mobilities of the solute
and the anionic component of the background electrolyte.
Thus in order to improve peak symmetry, butyric, hepta-
noic, octanoic, nonanoic, decanoic, and mandelic acids
were tested as anionic components of the buffer. The
buffer concentration was kept to 12.5 mM with ammonium
as buffer cation in an ethanol–methanol mixture (40:60).
The results are presented in Table 7.
As expected the use of long-chain buffer anions which
present a lower electrophoretic mobility led to an improve-
ment of peak symmetry and therefore of efficiency. Enan-
tioselectivity was not significantly modified. Nonanoic acid
was added at the same concentration in an ethanol–
methanol mixture (60:40) and gave higher a and Rs values
but lower As. Finally, a buffer made of 12.5 mM octanoic
acid and ammonia in the same ethanol–methanol mixture
(60:40) gave the best results (for DNB-Leu: a = 1.031, Rs =
4.5, N = 145,000, As = 1.0) and was selected for further
experiments. Figure 5 shows the electropherograms ob-
tained for DNB-Leu and DNB-Phg under these conditions.

Influence of the Concentration of the Acidic Compo-
nent of Background Electrolyte and the pHa.
ammonium octanoate background electrolyte in methanol–
ethanol (40:60), SO concentrations #10 mM could be used
without current instability. By keeping the ammonia con-
centration at 12.5 mM and increasing tBuCQN concentra-
tion to 5 mM, the concentration of octanoic acid was in-
creased and the resulting pHa was measured. Table 8
presents the effect of octanoic acid concentration. An im-
portant gain in a appeared with increasing concentration.
Maximum selectivity was obtained at a 200 mM concentra-
tion of octanoic acid but peak efficiency and symmetry
were decreased under these conditions. µe and particularly
µEOF were also found to decrease with the pHa, resulting in
higher migration times. The 100 mM acid concentration
was the best compromise in terms of selectivity, resolution,
efficiency, symmetry, and analysis time. Using 12.5 mM
ammonia, 100 mM octanoic acid, and 10 mM tBuCQN,
DNB-Leu enantiomers could be separated with a = 1.572,
Rs = 64.3, N = 127,000, and an asymmetry factor of 0.93 for
the first enantiomer. Figure 6 shows the enantioseparation
of DNB-Phe, DNB-Leu, and DNB-aAbu using these opti-
A NACE system using a background electrolyte made of
12.5 mM of ammonium acetate in methanol was found to
be useful for the investigation of the potential of QN and
one of its derivatives tBuCQN as SO for the enantiosepa-
ration of N-protected amino acids. Enantioselectivity im-
proved when DNB-derivatives were tested as SAs and
tBuCQN was used as SO. Reducing the capillary tempera-
ture had a favorable effect on enantioselectivity but the
enantioseparation was not significantly modified by
changes of the applied voltage. However, the best peak
efficiency and symmetry were observed at −25 kV. There-
fore, this voltage was selected for further investigations.
The addition of acetonitrile to the methanolic background
electrolyte led to an increase in migration times and a de-
crease in selectivity and efficiency. The addition of ethanol
to the methanolic background electrolyte did not affect
migration times significantly and, in contrast to acetoni-
trile, gave rise to higher selectivity. Peak efficiency and
symmetry were also improved. After studying the influence
of the nature and concentration of the buffer anion, show-
ing that a low pHa was favorable to enantioselectivity, the
most suitable composition for the background electrolyte
was found to 100 mM octanoic acid and 12.5 mM ammonia
in a methanol–ethanol mixture (40:60) containing 10 mM
tBuCQN. Using these experimental conditions, this kind of
NACE system could easily be applied to the investigation
of other QN derivatives as SOs.
